context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Civil Society: NGOs and Social Movements

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
There is no doubt about the mission of NGOs for promoting development. How good are NGOs as development agents? In what circumstances are they more effective as agents of development? What are their limitations? What is the role of other civil society actors in development such as social movements? What is the boundary of civil society as an actor in particular development contexts?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection about NGOs and civil society in development, and I’m not totally sure how to answer the prompt in a balanced way. I think NGOs can help, but I’m confused about how to explain when they’re effective versus their limitations.
exchanges.tutor: Good start — being aware that the answer needs to be balanced is already the right instinct. Let me ask you this: from your readings or general knowledge, can you think of one concrete example where an NGO made a real difference in a community? What made it work in that case?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the start of the assignment and seems generally oriented but unsure how to structure a balanced argument. Rather than giving them a framework, I should ask a probing question that gets them thinking about what they already know, using the Socratic method to activate prior knowledge before diving into the nuances of the prompt.

exchanges.student: I think maybe it works when an NGO is close to the community and actually listens to local people instead of just bringing in an outside plan. I’m not sure of a specific case though—could I use a general example like an NGO helping with local health or education programs, or do I really need one named case?
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid observation — the idea of local embeddedness and responsiveness is actually quite central to debates about NGO effectiveness. On examples: a general one can work, but your argument will be much stronger if it's grounded in something specific. Have you come across any cases in your course readings that might fit?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuinely good observation about proximity and listening to local communities - this is a substantive insight worth acknowledging. On the specific example question, I should redirect them back to their readings rather than letting them drift into vague generalities, as the course emphasizes case studies and real-world examples. I'll validate their insight and nudge them toward grounding it better.

exchanges.student: I’m blanking a little on the readings, honestly. I vaguely remember the course talking more broadly about participation, decentralization, and external actors, so maybe I could use that instead of forcing a case I don’t remember well?
exchanges.tutor: Those course themes — participation, decentralization, external actors — are definitely relevant here. But rather than replacing a concrete example, they can help you *frame* one. Think about it this way: the prompt asks about *circumstances* where NGOs are more effective. Based on your insight about local embeddedness, what conditions would need to be in place for that to work? Start there and the example may come more naturally.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to avoid engaging with specific evidence, which could weaken their reflection. However, they've made a legitimate connection to course themes like participation and external actors, which is relevant. I should encourage them to work with what they remember from the readings rather than letting them off the hook entirely, while also pushing them to think more concretely about the prompt's specific questions.

exchanges.student: Okay, I think the conditions might be: the NGO works with local institutions, the community actually participates, and the state isn’t totally absent or hostile. I’m still a little unsure about the state part though, because NGOs are sometimes supposed to help where the state is weak, right?
exchanges.tutor: You've just put your finger on one of the central tensions in this debate — good. So let me ask: is there a difference between a state being *weak* and a state being *hostile* to NGO work? Does that distinction change how you think about the role NGOs can play?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has generated three solid conditions and is now grappling with a genuine tension in the literature - the relationship between NGOs and the state. This is exactly the kind of productive confusion that should be encouraged. Rather than resolving the tension for them, I should ask a question that helps them think through it themselves, as this tension is actually key to a nuanced reflection.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I think so. If the state is weak, maybe NGOs can fill gaps in services for a while, but if the state is hostile, then NGOs probably can’t do much or they might lose legitimacy. I’m not fully sure if “fill gaps” is the right way to phrase it, though.
exchanges.tutor: That distinction is solid — don't get too hung up on the exact phrasing, that's for you to work out in your writing. You've now got some good material on effectiveness and conditions. The prompt also asks about NGO *limitations* and the role of *social movements*. How do you see social movements as different from NGOs in how they pursue development goals?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a genuinely good conceptual distinction between weak and hostile states. Their hesitation about 'fill gaps' is worth addressing briefly, but I don't want to dwell on phrasing since my role is not to polish their writing. More importantly, the student has been building good material for several exchanges and is now at a point where they should start thinking about limitations and the other civil society actors mentioned in the prompt, to avoid dwelling too long on one aspect.

exchanges.student: I think NGOs are usually more organized and project-based, while social movements are more political and push for broader structural change. But I’m not sure if that’s too simplistic. Could you help me make that distinction sharper?
exchanges.tutor: That's a meaningful distinction, not too simplistic at all. Here's a question to sharpen it yourself: can you think of a situation where that difference in approach — project-based vs. structural/political — might actually make NGOs *less* effective than a social movement at achieving lasting change?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuinely useful distinction and is asking me to sharpen it for them - but that would cross into doing the intellectual work for them. Instead, I should validate the direction and ask a question that pushes them to deepen it themselves.

exchanges.student: Maybe when the problem is rooted in power, like land rights or labor exploitation, an NGO project might only treat the symptoms, while a social movement can pressure the state or employers to change the system. I think that makes sense, but I’m worried I’m making NGOs sound useless now.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong point about structural vs. symptomatic change. And your worry is actually useful — it means you're thinking about balance. So ask yourself: does criticizing NGOs for not addressing root causes mean they have *no* value, or does it mean their value is more *limited* and *context-dependent* than often claimed?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made an excellent analytical point about structural vs. symptomatic approaches. Their worry about making NGOs sound useless is actually a sign of good critical thinking about balance. Rather than reassuring them, I should turn this back to them - the tension they feel is exactly what makes for a nuanced reflection, and they should work through it themselves.

exchanges.student: I think it means their value is more limited and context-dependent, not that they’re useless. So maybe my main argument is that NGOs can be effective in service delivery, participation, and advocacy in some settings, but they have limits when development problems are tied to state power, inequality, or long-term institution building.
exchanges.tutor: That's a well-formed central argument — you built it yourself, which is exactly how it should work. One piece of the prompt you haven't touched yet: what do you think the *boundary* of civil society is as an actor in development? In other words, what can't civil society do, no matter how well-organized it is?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a clear, well-structured thesis through their own reasoning - this is a significant milestone. I should acknowledge this genuinely without being excessive, and now push them to think about the final piece of the prompt they haven't fully addressed: the boundary of civil society as an actor in particular development contexts. This will help them complete their thinking before writing.

exchanges.student: I think civil society can influence, pressure, deliver services, and represent community interests, but it can’t fully replace the state in making and enforcing policy at a large scale. I’m a little unsure how to phrase the “boundary” part without sounding too absolute.
exchanges.tutor: You've now worked through all the key parts of the prompt. Don't overthink the phrasing — that's something you'll work out as you write. You have a clear argument and enough substance to fill 700–800 words. Try writing a first draft using what you've developed here, then you can come back if you get stuck on something specific.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now covered all the major components of the prompt through their own reasoning: NGO effectiveness, conditions, limitations, social movements, and civil society boundaries. They have enough material to write. Their concern about phrasing is a writing task they need to handle themselves. I should not help with phrasing, but I can push them to start writing and integrate what they've developed in this conversation.

exchanges.student: Thanks, that helps. I’ll try drafting around that main argument, and if I get stuck I might come back with my intro or thesis because that part still feels awkward.
exchanges.tutor: Good — go write. One reminder for when you come back: I can help you think through your ideas, but the wording, structure, and phrasing are entirely yours to figure out. Bring your draft and we'll work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is wrapping up and signaling they'll return with a draft. I should encourage them to go write, but also set a clear boundary: I can help them think through ideas but will not edit, polish, or suggest language for their writing. This is a good moment to remind them of that boundary proactively.
